coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of kappa fiber modified graphite carbon nitride with outstanding photocatalytic phenol degradation ability
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shaozheng
Wang, Hui
Wang, Fei
Li, Zheng
Li, Wei
Bai, Jin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, kapok fiber (KF) modified graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was prepared by pyrolysis method and the photocatalytic degradation ability of phenol was investigated. XRD, UV–Vis DRS, FT-IR, TEM, XPS, N2 adsorption and PL were used to characterize the as-prepared catalysts. The results showed that KF, as a dual-function modifier, not only provided carbon source for carbon doping, but transformed into activated carbon substrate during calcination, making g-C3N4 grow on it. The KF modified g-C3N4 displays larger specific surface area, thinner layers, stronger visible light absorption capacity, higher separation efficiency and photocatalytic degradation performance. The KF modified g-C3N4 shows the phenol degradation rate constant of 0.258 h−1, which is 4.2 times higher than that of the neat g-C3N4, as well as excellent catalytic stability and structural stability. The excessive calcination temperature results in the low content of g-C3N4 in the catalyst, leading to the decline of photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Kapok fiber
Phenol
Photocatalysis
Pollutant degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of catalysts with different KF content (a) or different calcination temperature (b).
FT-IR spectra of catalysts with different KF content (a) or different calcination temperature (b).

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance specta of catalysts with different KF content (a) or different calcination temperature (b) and the plots of the transformed Kubelka-Munk function versus the energy of light (c and d).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of as-prepared catalysts.
TEM images of CN(600) (a), KF(1%)-CN(600) (b), KF(5%)-CN(600) (c) and KF(10%)-CN(600) (d).
XPS spectra of CN(600) and KF(5%)-CN(600) in the region of C 1 s (a) and N 1 s (b).
PL and EIS spectra of catalysts with different KF content (a and c) or different calcination temperature (b and d).

Phenol degradation rate of catalysts with different KF content under visible light (a), the relationship between -ln(C/C0) and reaction time (b), phenol degradation rate of catalysts with different calcination temperature under visible light (c), photocatalytic degradation rate of other phenolic compounds over CN(600) and KF(5%)-CN(600) (d), phenol degradation rate of KF(5%)-CN(600) and carbon doped catalysts prepared by previous work (e) and TOC removal rate of as-prepared catalysts (f).
The schematic diagram of photocatalytic degradation mechanism of KF modified g-C3N4.
Photocatalytic stability (a) and TEM image (b) of KF(5%)-CN(600), UV–Vis (c) and PL (d) results of fresh and reused KF(5%)-CN(600).
The elemental analysis results of as-prepared catalysts.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of kappa fiber modified graphite carbon nitride with outstanding photocatalytic phenol degradation ability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun 113001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering, and Environmental Engineering, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun 113001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, kapok fiber (KF) modified graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was prepared by pyrolysis method and the photocatalytic degradation ability of phenol was investigated. XRD, UV–Vis DRS, FT-IR, TEM, XPS, N2 adsorption and PL were used to characterize the as-prepared catalysts. The results showed that KF, as a dual-function modifier, not only provided carbon source for carbon doping, but transformed into activated carbon substrate during calcination, making g-C3N4 grow on it. The KF modified g-C3N4 displays larger specific surface area, thinner layers, stronger visible light absorption capacity, higher separation efficiency and photocatalytic degradation performance. The KF modified g-C3N4 shows the phenol degradation rate constant of 0.258 h−1, which is 4.2 times higher than that of the neat g-C3N4, as well as excellent catalytic stability and structural stability. The excessive calcination temperature results in the low content of g-C3N4 in the catalyst, leading to the decline of photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: With the depletion of traditional fossil energy, the new energy represented by solar energy is gradually entering the stage of history. Photocatalysis technology provides an effective way for the conversion and utilization of solar energy [1–5]. In recent years, graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a nonmetallic semiconductor photocatalyst, has gained more and more researchers' attention [6,7]. g-C3N4 has many unique advantages, such as wide source of precursor and cheap price, simple preparation method, no heavy metal pollution, good chemical stability, moderate energy band structure, etc. However, g-C3N4 also has similar disadvantages to other photocatalysts, such as high electron-hole recombination rate, low specific surface area and low visible light utilization [8,9]. In view of these shortcomings, the structure of g-C3N4 based photocatalyst was improved by various methods, such as metal/nonmetal doping, heterojunction construction, and surface modification [10–15]. Carbon self-doping is one of the effective ways to improve the performance of g-C3N4 based photocatalyst. According to literatures, carbon doped g-C3N4 prepared from chemical carbon sources, such as polyacrylamide [16], glucose [17] and cellulose acetic acid [18], can greatly improve the efficiency of charge separation and visible light utilization. However, the introduction of chemical carbon sources has the risk of polluting the environment. There are also many reports concerning g-C3N4 modified by carbon quantum dots [19–24]. However, the preparation method of carbon quantum dot is complicated, leading to the high cost of the as-prepared catalyst. The studies on the modification of g-C3N4 using biomass as carbon source are rarely reported.

Nature is the largest repository of materials in which the primitive forms of all man-made materials, such as core-shell, flower, honeycomb, tubular or fibrous structures, can be found. Therefore, in the structural design of materials, researchers tend to imitate nature or seek biological templates from nature. Kapok fiber (KF) is a versatile biomass material, mainly used for interior decoration, bedding, sound insulation and heat insulation, but also can be used as the filler of life buoy. In recent years, KF has been used more and more because of its light weight, antibacterial, anti-mite and other excellent characteristics. Compared with cotton and synthetic fibers, KF has the highest hollow ratio of 97%. As a catalyst, this property provides a larger specific surface area and a more convenient channel for charge transfer [25].

Phenol wastewater mainly comes from petrochemical industry, coal chemical industry and pharmaceutical industry. There are mainly three kinds of phenols in phenolic wastewater, phenol, catechol and chlorophenol [26–28]. With the development of industry and economy, phenolic wastewater has become one of the most important pollutants in water. Phenol, as a representative substance of phenolic compounds, is listed as carcinogenic dangerous goods [28]. Therefore, the oxidative degradation of phenol wastewater has always been an important research topic. In this work, kapok fiber (KF) modified g-C3N4 was prepared by pyrolysis method and their photocatalytic degradation ability of phenol was investigated. The effect of KF concentration and calcination temperature on photocatalytic properties of as-prepared catalyst was investigated.

1 g of dicyandiamide was dissolved into 15 mL of deionized water, then KF with mass ratios of 1%, 5% and 10% was added, respectively. The suspension was stirred vigorously for 30 min, and heated to 100 °C under stirring to remove the water. The solid was dried in oven thoroughly and ground into powder. Then, the powder was put into muffle furnace and annealed at 600 °C for 2 h with the rate of 5 °C·min−1. The obtained catalyst was named as KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600). Dicyandiamide was calcined at 600 °C for 2 h with the rate of 5 °C·min−1 and the obtained neat g-C3N4 was named as CN(600). In order to investigate the effect of calcination temperature on the performance of the catalyst, the calcination temperature of the KF(5%)-CN(600) was changed to 550 °C and 650 °C respectively, and the resulting catalysts were named as KF(5%)-CN(550) and KF(5%)-CN(650).

X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) was carried out by X-ray diffractometer (XRD-7000, Shimadzu). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured at −196 °C using a Micrometrics ASAP 2010 physical adsorber. Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was measured by UV–Vis spectrometer (UV-550 JASCA). The morphology of the catalyst was characterized by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips Tecnai G220). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using a Thermo ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer. The fluorescence spectrum (PL) was tested by an FP-6300 fluorescence spectrophotometer using Xe lamp as an excitation source.

Phenol photodegradation was investigated using a self-made photocatalytic reactor. 0.100 g of the catalyst was dispersed into 200 mL of phenol aqueous solution with the concentration of 10 ppm and stirred for 30 min. The high pressure sodium lamp with the power of 250 W (400 < λ < 800 nm) was used as the visible light source. The reaction was carried out at 30 °C and atmospheric pressure. Air was bubbled during the reaction. 5 mL of the suspension were collected at given time intervals. The concentration of phenol was determined by 4-aminoantipyrine photometry.

Fig. 1a shows the XRD patterns of CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600). All the catalysts showed two diffraction peaks at 13° and 27.7°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of the g-C3N4 respectively [29,30]. No other diffraction peaks can be seen in Fig. 1a. With the increase of KF content, the positions of the two diffraction peaks do not change, but the peak intensity significantly decreases. This may be due to the formation of large amount of activated carbon during the KF calcination. Fig. 1b compares the effect of different calcination temperatures on the XRD pattern of the catalyst. It can be seen that there is no significant difference in the peak positions of the three catalysts, and the peak strength of KF(5%)-CN(650) is slightly weaker than that of the other two catalysts. This may be due to the excessive calcination temperature caused the dicyandiamide decomposition, leading to the low g-C3N4 content in KF(5%)-CN(650).

The FT-IR spectra of CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600) are displayed in Fig. 2a. The absorption peaks are mainly concentrated in three regions: 810 cm−1, 1240–1650 cm−1 and 3100–3500 cm−1, which are consistent with previous reports [31]. They are attributed to the flexural vibration absorption of triazine ring compounds, the stretching vibration absorption of aromatic heterocyclic compounds, and the stretching vibration absorption of NH bond formed by incomplete condensation. In addition, it can be clearly seen in Fig. 2a that with the increase of KF content, an obvious absorption peak appears at 1210 cm−1, which should be assigned to the CC bond of formed activated carbon. Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectra of catalysts prepared under different temperature. It can be seen that the absorption peak near 810 cm−1 moves to higher wavenumber gradually with the increase of calcination temperature. This result indicates that with the increase of calcination temperature, the interaction between the generated activated carbon and g-C3N4 is gradually enhanced.

Fig. 3a displays the UV–Vis spectra of CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600). The absorption boundary for CN(600) locates at 470 nm. After KF modification, the absorption boundary of the catalyst shifts significantly to the higher wavelength. The band gaps are estimated from the tangent lines in the plots of the square root of the Kubelka-Munk functions against the photon energy (Fig. 3c). The result shows that the band gaps for CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600) are 2.64, 2.53, 2.49 and 2.47 eV. The decrease of band gap energy may be caused by the carbon doping during the calcination process [32]. In addition, it can be seen that with the increase of KF content, the absorption of the catalyst in the region of 500–800 nm gradually increases, and the color of the corresponding catalyst also gradually deepens. This is due to the increasing content of activated carbon in the catalyst.

Fig. 3b shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of catalysts prepared under different temperature. The absorption boundaries of the three catalysts are basically the same, indicating that the calcination temperature has no effect on the band gap energy of the catalyst (Fig. 3d). Compared with KF(5%)-CN(550) and KF(5%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(650) displays the better adsorption ability to the light with a wavelength >500 nm. This may be due to the excessive calcination temperature caused dicyandiamide decomposition, leading to the content of g-C3N4 in KF(5%)-CN(650) decreased, while the relative content of activated carbon increased. This is consistent with the XRD result. The C/N ratio was obtained using elemental analysis (Table 1 ). The result shows that the C/N ratios for CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600), KF(10%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(550) and KF(5%)-CN(650) are 0.74, 0.755, 0.792, 0.858, 0.795 and 0.826. This hints that the C/N ratio increased significantly with the increase of KF content. This is due to that KF calcined into activated carbon, resulting in a higher C/N ratio. KF(5%)-CN(650) exhibits an obviously higher C/N ratio than that of KF(5%)-CN(550) and KF(5%)-CN(600). This confirmed the above speculation, that is, the excessive temperature caused dicyandiamide decomposition, leading to the content of g-C3N4 in the product decreased, while the relative content of activated carbon increased.

Fig. 4 displays the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of as-prepared catalysts. All samples show type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H4 hysteresis loop [33–35]. These pores may be caused by the accumulation of catalyst particles on each other. The specific surface areas for CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600), KF(10%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(550) and KF(5%)-CN(650) are calculated to be 9.2, 16.8, 25.5, 29.6, 27.2 and 20.7 m2·g−1. This result shows that KF content has a significant effect on the specific surface area of the catalyst. The promoted calcination temperature decreases the specific surface area of the catalyst with the same KF content.

Fig. 5 displays the TEM images of CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600). CN(600) has a dark color (Fig. 5a), indicating that it is thick and is made up of many layers of g-C3N4. In the case of KF modified sample, the color of the catalyst becomes lighter, hinting the thinner layers of the catalyst. In addition, in Fig. 5b–d, many of the banding edges are clearly visible, and the number of these nanoribbons gradually increases with increasing the KF content. It is deduced that these nanoribbons may be the formed activated carbon. These activated carbon nanoribbons serve as the substrate for g-C3N4 epitaxial growth, leading to the decreased interaction force between the g-C3N4 layers. Thus, the catalyst thinned and the specific surface area increased.

XPS spectra were used to investigate the electronic state of surface elements. Fig. 6a shows the XPS spectra of CN(600) and KF(5%)-CN(600) in the region of C 1s. Three peaks located at 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.2 eV are observed for CN(600). They are respectively attributed to the polluting carbon source, the terminal C-NHx structure and N=C–N structure in the aromatic ring [36]. The C 1s spectrum of KF(5%)-CN(600) has the same peak positions as that of CN(600), indicating that the introduction of KF has no significant effect on the electron state of carbon. In addition, it is worth noting that the peak intensity at 284.6 eV for KF(5%)-CN(600) is significantly greater than that of CN(600). This result should be attributed to the formation of CC bond in the activated carbons. As shown in Fig. 6b, two binding energies are shown for CN(600) in the region of N 1s. They are located at 398.3 eV and 400.2 eV, which should be attributed to the C-N=C and N-(C)3 in g-C3N4 [36]. The binding energy of the N 1 s orbital of KF(5%)-CN(600) is shifted significantly compared to that of CN(600). This is due to the doping of carbon into g-C3N4 lattice which changes the chemical environment of nitrogen atoms.

The PL spectra of as-prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 7 . All the catalysts display fluorescence emission peaks around 460 nm, which is consistent with the absorption boundary in the UV–Vis spectra. In Fig. 7a, the PL intensity follows the order: CN(600) > KF(1%)-CN(600) > KF(5%)-CN(600) > KF(10%)-CN(600). The higher the KF content, the weaker the PL spectral peak. The fluorescence emission peak of PL is known to come from the quenching of electron-hole pair, that is, the higher the electron-hole pair recombination rate, the stronger the PL peak intensity [37]. This hints that the introduction of KF promote the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. This may be due to, on the one hand, carbon atoms of KF are doped into the catalyst lattice during the calcination process [32]; on the other hand, the activated carbon substrate generated by KF calcination can quickly transfer the photogenerated charge, inhibiting the recombination. Fig. 7b shows the PL spectra of catalysts prepared under different temperature. It can be seen that, with the increase of calcination temperature, the PL intensity of the catalyst decreases, that is, the electron-hole separation efficiency increases. This may be due to that, with the increase of calcination temperature, the interaction between g-C3N4 and activated carbon substrate is gradually enhanced, which is favorable for the transmission of photogenerated charge. The effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is further confirmed by EIS spectra (Fig. 7c and d). CN(600) exhibits the largest arc radius suggesting a largest resistance of working electrodes, which is harmful to the charge transmission. The decreased arc radius for KF modified catalyst hints the promoted effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which is consistent with the PL result.

Fig. 8a shows the phenol degradation rate of catalysts with different KF content under visible light. The phenol degradation rate for CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600) are 23%, 43%, 69% and 63% in 5 h. This hints that the addition of KF can promote the phenol degradation rate of as-prepared catalysts. KF(10%)-CN(600) exhibits the slight lower phenol degradation rate than that of KF(5%)-CN(600), which is probably due to the lower g-C3N4 content in KF(10%)-CN(600). The first order kinetic model was used to calculate the rate constant of phenol degradation reaction, and the results are shown in Fig. 8b [38]. The rate constants for CN(600), KF(1%)-CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) and KF(10%)-CN(600) are 0.061 h−1, 0.127 h−1, 0.258 h−1 and 0.21 h−1. The rate constant of KF(5%)-CN(600) is 4.2 times that of CN(600).

Fig. 8c shows the phenol degradation rate of catalysts with different calcination temperature under visible light. KF(5%)-CN(600) displays the higher phenol degradation rate than that of KF(5%)-CN(550). This is due to that, with the increase of calcination temperature, the interaction between activated carbon substrate and g-C3N4 is enhanced, which is beneficial to the migration of photogenerated charge. The electron-hole separation efficiency and visible light adsorption ability of KF(5%)-CN(650) is better than that of KF(5%)-CN(600), but its phenol degradation rate is lower than that of the latter. g-C3N4 should be the main catalyst in the composite. It is reported in previous work that g-C3N4 is basically stable at 600 °C, whereas about 20% of the weight is lost as the temperature rises to 650 °C [26]. Thus, the decreased activity of KF(5%)-CN(650) may be caused by the excessive calcination temperature during the preparation of the catalyst, which resulted in dicyandiamide decomposition, leading to a decreased g-C3N4 content in the composite catalyst. In addition, the specific surface area of KF(5%)-CN(650) is lower than that of KF(5%)-CN(600), which is bad for the photocatalytic performance.

It is known from the above characterization results that the higher KF content and calcination temperature, the higher electron-hole separation efficiency. Thus, KF(10%)-CN(650) was also prepared and used for phenol photodegradation. Its phenol degradation rate is 51%, lower than those of KF(5%)-CN(600). This may be also caused by the excessive calcination temperature, leading to the decreased g-C3N4 content in the catalyst. In addition, the specific surface area of KF(10%)-CN(650), measured by N2 adsorption, is 22.9 m2·g−1. This value is much lower than that of KF(10%)-CN(600). In order to further prove the improved degradation ability of KF modified catalyst to phenolic compounds, two other phenolic compounds catechol and 2-chlorophenol were selected as the reaction substrates. As shown in Fig. 8d, compared with CN(600), KF(5%)-CN(600) shows the better degradation rate on both phenolic compounds. This confirms the addition of KF can promote the degradation rate of phenolic compounds over g-C3N4 catalyst. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of KF(5%)-CN(600) and carbon doped catalysts prepared by previous work is compared [16–18]. The result shows that KF(5%)-CN(600) exhibit the better phenol degradation ability than that of other catalysts, confirming its excellent photocatalytic performance (Fig. 8e). In addition to the better photocatalytic performance, the preparation method of KF(5%)-CN(600) is much simpler than those in the literatures [16–18]. The TOC removal rate is also investigated and the result is shown in Fig. 8f. The KF modified catalysts show obvious promoted TOC removal rate than that of CN(600).

In a word, as a dual-function modifier, KF not only provided carbon source for carbon doping, but transformed into activated carbon substrate during calcination, making g-C3N4 grow on it. When KF modified catalyst is excited by visible light, more electron-hole pairs are formed due to the lower bang gap energy. The strong interaction between activated carbon substrate and g-C3N4 causes that the charges can migrate rapidly between them, leading to the reduced electron-hole recombination rate. The photoelectrons and holes are converted into strong oxidizing species, which oxidize the phenol to carbon dioxide and water, as shown in Fig. 9 . KF(5%)-CN(600) was reused for 5 times, and the reaction results are shown in Fig. 10a. It can be seen that the degradation rate of phenol does not change significantly after five times. As shown in Fig. 10b, the catalyst after 5 cycles displays the similar morphology to the fresh catalyst. In addition, after 5 cycles, the C/N ratio of KF(5%)-CN(600) is 0.793, which is almost the same as the fresh catalyst (0.792). No obvious difference between fresh and reused KF(5%)-CN(600) is shown in UV–Vis and PL results (Fig. 10c and d). All these results indicate that the KF modified g-C3N4 has excellent catalytic stability and structural stability.

In this work, kapok fiber (KF) modified g-C3N4 was prepared by pyrolysis method and the photocatalytic degradation ability of phenol was investigated. The results showed that KF, as a dual-function modifier, not only provided carbon source for carbon doping, but transformed into activated carbon substrate during calcination, making g-C3N4 grow on it. The KF modified g-C3N4 displays larger specific surface area, thinner layers and stronger visible light absorption capacity. In addition, the generated activated carbon substrate is beneficial to the migration of photogenerated charge, which greatly improves the electron-hole separation efficiency and the photocatalytic degradation performance. In the preparation process, the increase of calcination temperature is beneficial to enhance the interaction between activated carbon substrate and g-C3N4, causing the promoted charge separation efficiency. However, the excessive calcination temperature resulted in the low content of g-C3N4 in the catalyst, leading to the decline of photocatalytic performance. KF(5%)-CN(600) shows the highest phenol degradation rate constant of 0.258 h−1, which is 4.2 times higher than that of the neat g-C3N4, as well as excellent catalytic stability and structural stability.

Shaozheng Hu: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Hui Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Fei Wang: Methodology. Zheng Li: Validation. Wei Li: Formal analysis. Jin Bai: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Department of Education of Liaoning Province (No. L2014145) and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (201602467).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An advanced semimetal-organic bi spheres-g-C3N4 nanohybrid with SPR-enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance for NO purification
Environ. Sci. Technol.
